nodes.text: Good evening. Take a deep breath. Let's look back at your day.
When you replay the most critical moment of your day, where was the primary friction?

options.text: The expectations placed on me were unclear or unrealistic.
I realized I hadn't prepared enough for the reality of the situation.
Other people's delays or mistakes forced me to scramble.
I made a strategic error in how I approached it.

nodes.text: You're taking ownership of the friction. In the moment you realized the gap in your preparation or strategy, what did you do?

options.text: I paused, recalibrated, and changed my approach.
I pushed through using brute force, hoping it would work out.
I asked for specific feedback or help to bridge the gap.
I felt overwhelmed but kept it to myself.

nodes.text: It's exhausting when friction comes from the environment. When you hit that wall, how did you try to move forward?

options.text: I waited for someone to clarify or unblock me.
I focused strictly on what I could control in my immediate radius.
I voiced my frustration so people knew what I was dealing with.
I negotiated a new boundary or deadline to protect the work.

nodes.text: It sounds like you held onto your agency today. Even when things went off-script, you looked for the levers you could actually pull.
It’s completely normal to feel stuck when external forces derail you. But noticing your reaction to that chaos is the first step to reclaiming control.
We've looked at how you handled friction. Now let's look at how you interacted with the people around you.
Think about a moment today where you felt out of sync with a colleague. What was the underlying feeling?

options.text: I felt they should have recognized how much effort I was putting in.
I wondered if I had communicated my needs clearly enough.
I felt annoyed that I was carrying more than my fair share.
I wanted to understand what was blocking them from engaging.

nodes.text: You approached the disconnect with curiosity. What did that cost you in the moment?

options.text: It took extra emotional labor when I was already tired.
It meant I had to slow down my own work to bring them along.
Honestly, I felt resentful even while trying to be helpful.
Nothing—it felt like the most efficient way to solve the problem.

nodes.text: It’s natural to want fairness and recognition. When you felt that imbalance, what was your default assumption?

options.text: That they don't value my time or standards as much as I do.
That I need to stop doing extra work if it's not appreciated.
That maybe they are dealing with invisible pressures I don't see.
That the system or management is failing to hold people accountable.

nodes.text: You showed up with a mindset of giving today. That discretionary effort is the invisible glue that holds teams together.
When we focus heavily on what we are owed, every interaction becomes a transaction. Shifting focus to what you can give often reduces that internal friction.
You've reflected on your agency and your orientation toward others. Finally, let's zoom out to your radius of concern.
When the stress peaked today, how wide was your lens?

options.text: I was entirely focused on surviving my own task list.
I was worried about how my performance would be perceived.
I was thinking about how our team's bottleneck affects the next department.
I was focused on making sure the client didn't feel our internal chaos.

nodes.text: When we are overwhelmed, our radius naturally shrinks to protect ourselves. If you had possessed 10% more capacity today, where would you have directed it?

options.text: To finally catch up and give myself some breathing room.
To proactively check in on a teammate who was also struggling.
To plan ahead so tomorrow wouldn't feel so chaotic.
To fix a broken process that affects everyone.

nodes.text: Holding a wide radius is powerful but heavy. How did holding that broader perspective affect you?

options.text: It gave the stress meaning—I knew *why* the work mattered.
It actually amplified my anxiety because I felt responsible for everything.
It helped me separate what was actually urgent from what was just noise.
It made me ignore my own boundaries until I was depleted.

nodes.text: Stress inevitably narrows our focus. But remember: expanding your view to see how you fit into the larger system can sometimes lighten the load.
You managed to keep the bigger picture in view. That broader perspective—self-transcendence—is a hallmark of resilience.
Thank you for engaging deeply. Let's summarize where you are today.

Today's snapshot: You leaned towards an {axis1_dom} locus of control, a mindset of {axis2_dom}, and a {axis3_dom} radius of concern. These aren't permanent traits—they're choices you get to make again tomorrow.
Rest well. See you tomorrow.
